infobox.key: Next [House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") election
infobox.value: [November 3, 2026](/wiki/2026_United_States_House_of_Representatives_elections "2026 United States House of Representatives elections")

* [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") (217) * [Independent](/wiki/Independent_Republican_(United_States) "Independent Republican (United States)") ([1](/wiki/Kevin_Kiley_(politician) "Kevin Kiley (politician)"))   **Minority (214)**   * [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") (214)   **Vacant (3)**   * Vacant (3) |

is the [legislative branch](/wiki/Legislature "Legislature") of the [federal government of the United States](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States"). It is a [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") legislature, including a [lower body](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house"), the [U.S. House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"), and an [upper body](/wiki/Upper_house "Upper house"), the [U.S. Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"). They both meet in the [United States Capitol](/wiki/United_States_Capitol "United States Capitol") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")

The **United States Congress** is the [legislative branch](/wiki/Legislature "Legislature") of the [federal government of the United States](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States"). It is a [bicameral](/wiki/Bicameralism "Bicameralism") legislature, including a [lower body](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house"), the [U.S. House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives"), and an [upper body](/wiki/Upper_house "Upper house"), the [U.S. Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate"). They both meet in the [United States Capitol](/wiki/United_States_Capitol "United States Capitol") in [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.")

Members of Congress are chosen through [direct election](/wiki/Direct_election "Direct election"),[[b]](#cite_note-17A-3) though vacancies in the Senate may be filled by a [governor](/wiki/Governor_(United_States) "Governor (United States)")'s appointment. Congress has a total of 535 voting members, which includes 100 [senators](/wiki/List_of_current_United_States_senators "List of current United States senators") and 435 [representatives](/wiki/List_of_current_United_States_representatives "List of current United States representatives"). The House of Representatives also has 6 additional [non-voting members](/wiki/Non-voting_members_of_the_United_States_House_of_Representatives "Non-voting members of the United States House of Representatives"). The [vice president of the United States](/wiki/Vice_president_of_the_United_States "Vice president of the United States"), as president of the Senate, has a vote in the Senate only when there is a tie.[[2]](#cite_note-4)

Congress[[c]](#cite_note-Style-6) convenes for a two-year [term](/wiki/Term_of_office "Term of office") (a Congress), commencing every other January. Each Congress is usually split into two sessions, one for each year. [Elections](/wiki/Elections_in_the_United_States "Elections in the United States") are held every even-numbered year on [Election Day](/wiki/Election_Day_(United_States) "Election Day (United States)"). The members of the House of Representatives are elected for the two-year term of a Congress. The [Reapportionment Act of 1929](/wiki/Reapportionment_Act_of_1929 "Reapportionment Act of 1929") established that there be 435 representatives, and the [Uniform Congressional District Act](/wiki/Uniform_Congressional_District_Act "Uniform Congressional District Act") requires that they be elected from [single-member constituencies](/wiki/Single-member_district "Single-member district") or *districts*. It is also required that the [congressional districts](/wiki/Congressional_districts_of_the_United_States "Congressional districts of the United States") be [apportioned](/wiki/United_States_congressional_apportionment "United States congressional apportionment") among [states](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") by [population](/wiki/Representation_by_population "Representation by population") every ten years using the [U.S. census](/wiki/United_States_census "United States census") results, provided that each state has at least one congressional representative. Each senator is elected [at-large](/wiki/At-large "At-large") in their state for a six-year term, with [terms staggered](/wiki/Staggered_elections "Staggered elections"), so every two years approximately one-third of the Senate is up for election. Each state, regardless of population or size, has two senators, so currently, there are 100 senators for the 50 states.

[Article One of the Constitution](/wiki/Article_One_of_the_U.S._Constitution "Article One of the U.S. Constitution") requires that members of Congress be at least 25 years old for the House and at least 30 years old for the Senate, be a [U.S. citizen](/wiki/Citizenship_of_the_United_States "Citizenship of the United States") for seven years for the House and nine years for the Senate, and be an inhabitant of the state which they represent. Members in both chambers may stand for re-election an unlimited number of times.

Congress was created by the [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States "Constitution of the United States")'s [First Article](/wiki/Article_One_of_the_United_States_Constitution "Article One of the United States Constitution") and first met in [1789](/wiki/1st_United_States_Congress "1st United States Congress"), replacing the [Congress of the Confederation](/wiki/Congress_of_the_Confederation "Congress of the Confederation") in its legislative function. Although not legally mandated, in practice members of Congress since the late 19th century are typically affiliated with one of the [two major parties](/wiki/Two-party_system "Two-party system"), the [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") or the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), and only rarely with a [third party](/wiki/Third-party_members_of_the_United_States_House_of_Representatives "Third-party members of the United States House of Representatives") or [independents](/wiki/Independent_politician "Independent politician") affiliated with no party. Members can also [switch parties](/wiki/Party_switching "Party switching") at any time, though this is uncommon.

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

An overview of the United States legislative process from the [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress")

[![Seven men wearing suits posing for a group picture.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Johnson_Impeachment_Committee.jpg/250px-Johnson_Impeachment_Committee.jpg)](/wiki/File:Johnson_Impeachment_Committee.jpg)

In 1868, this committee of representatives prosecuted [U.S. president](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [Andrew Johnson](/wiki/Andrew_Johnson "Andrew Johnson") in his impeachment trial, but the Senate did not convict him.

[Article One of the United States Constitution](/wiki/Article_One_of_the_United_States_Constitution "Article One of the United States Constitution") states, "All legislative Powers herein granted shall be vested in a Congress of the United States, which shall consist of a Senate and House of Representatives."[[4]](#cite_note-7) The House and Senate are equal partners in the legislative process – legislation cannot be enacted without the consent of both chambers.[[5]](#cite_note-8) The Constitution grants each chamber some unique powers. The Senate ratifies treaties and approves [presidential](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States") [appointments](/wiki/Appointments_Clause "Appointments Clause") while the House initiates [revenue](/wiki/Revenue "Revenue")-raising bills.[[6]](#cite_note-9)[[7]](#cite_note-10)

The House initiates and decides [impeachment](/wiki/Federal_impeachment_in_the_United_States "Federal impeachment in the United States") while the Senate votes on conviction and removal of office for impeachment cases.[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11) A two-thirds vote of the Senate is required before an impeached person can be removed from office.[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11)

The term *[Congress](/wiki/Congress "Congress")* can also refer to a particular meeting of the legislature. A Congress covers two years; the current one, the [119th Congress](/wiki/119th_United_States_Congress "119th United States Congress"), began on January 3, 2025, and will end on January 3, 2027. Since the adoption of the [Twentieth Amendment to the United States Constitution](/wiki/Twentieth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Twentieth Amendment to the United States Constitution"), the Congress has started and ended at noon on the third day of January of every odd-numbered year. Members of the Senate are referred to as senators, while members of the House of Representatives are commonly referred to as representatives, congressmen, or congresswomen.[[9]](#cite_note-12)

Congress provides both local representation of congressional districts by representatives and representation of states at-large by [senators](/wiki/US_Senators "US Senators").[[10]](#cite_note-13)

Scholar and representative [Lee H. Hamilton](/wiki/Lee_H._Hamilton "Lee H. Hamilton") asserted that the "historic mission of Congress has been to maintain freedom" and insisted it was a "driving force in American government"[[11]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14aa-14) and a "remarkably resilient institution".[[12]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11rtww-15) Congress is the "heart and soul of our democracy", according to this view, even though legislators rarely achieve the prestige or name recognition of presidents or [Supreme Court justices](/wiki/Supreme_court_justices "Supreme court justices"); one wrote that "legislators remain ghosts in America's historical imagination." One analyst argues that it is not a solely reactive institution but has played an active role in shaping government policy and is extraordinarily sensitive to public pressure.[[13]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14dd4-16) Several academics described Congress:

> Congress reflects us in all our strengths and all our weaknesses. It reflects our regional idiosyncrasies, our ethnic, religious, and racial diversity, our multitude of professions, and our shadings of opinion on everything from the value of war to the war over values. Congress is the government's most representative body ... Congress is essentially charged with reconciling our many points of view on the great public policy issues of the day.[[11]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14aa-14)

Congress is constantly changing and is constantly in flux.[[14]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14cc-17) In recent times, the [American South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") and [West](/wiki/American_west "American west") have [gained](/wiki/United_States_congressional_apportionment "United States congressional apportionment") House seats according to [demographic](/wiki/Demographic "Demographic") changes recorded by the census and includes more women and [minorities](/wiki/Minorities "Minorities").[[14]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14cc-17) Most [incumbents](/wiki/Incumbent "Incumbent") seek re-election, and their historical [likelihood of winning](/wiki/Incumbent#Incumbency_advantage "Incumbent") subsequent elections exceeds 90 percent.[[15]](#cite_note-incumbent-18)

While power balances among the different parts of [government](/wiki/Government "Government") continue to change, the internal structure of Congress is important to understand along with its interactions with so-called *intermediary institutions* such as [political parties](/wiki/Political_party "Political party"), [civic associations](/wiki/Civic_associations "Civic associations"), [interest groups](/wiki/Lobbying "Lobbying"), and the [mass media](/wiki/Mass_media "Mass media").[[13]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14dd4-16)

The [historical records of the House of Representatives and the Senate](/wiki/Congressional_archives "Congressional archives") are maintained by the Center for Legislative Archives, which is a part of the [National Archives and Records Administration](/wiki/National_Archives_and_Records_Administration "National Archives and Records Administration").[[16]](#cite_note-19)

Congress holds legislative authority over the [District of Columbia](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), as the current seat of the federal government. Despite the devolution of certain roles to municipal government, under the [Home Rule Act](/wiki/District_of_Columbia_Home_Rule_Act "District of Columbia Home Rule Act"), Congress has the power to 'review and disapprove' local laws before they take effect.[[17]](#cite_note-20)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the United States Congress](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_Congress "History of the United States Congress")

### 18th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 18th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Scene_at_the_Signing_of_the_Constitution_of_the_United_States.jpg/250px-Scene_at_the_Signing_of_the_Constitution_of_the_United_States.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scene_at_the_Signing_of_the_Constitution_of_the_United_States.jpg)

The 1940 portrait *[Scene at the Signing of the Constitution of the United States](/wiki/Scene_at_the_Signing_of_the_Constitution_of_the_United_States "Scene at the Signing of the Constitution of the United States")*, depicting [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") presiding over the signing of the [Constitution of the United States](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States "Constitution of the United States")

The [First Continental Congress](/wiki/First_Continental_Congress "First Continental Congress") was a gathering of representatives from twelve of the [Thirteen Colonies](/wiki/Thirteen_Colonies "Thirteen Colonies").[[18]](#cite_note-21) On July 4, 1776, the [Second Continental Congress](/wiki/Second_Continental_Congress "Second Continental Congress") adopted the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/Declaration_of_Independence_of_the_United_States "Declaration of Independence of the United States"), referring to the new nation as the "United States of America". The [Articles of Confederation](/wiki/Articles_of_Confederation "Articles of Confederation") in 1781 created the [Congress of the Confederation](/wiki/Congress_of_the_Confederation "Congress of the Confederation"), a [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameralism "Unicameralism") body with equal representation among the states in which each state had a [veto](/wiki/Veto "Veto") over most decisions. Congress had executive but not legislative authority, and the federal judiciary was confined to admiralty[[19]](#cite_note-22) and lacked authority to collect taxes, regulate commerce, or enforce laws.[[20]](#cite_note-23)[[21]](#cite_note-24)

Government powerlessness led to the [Convention of 1787](/wiki/Philadelphia_Convention "Philadelphia Convention") which proposed a revised constitution with a two-chamber or *bicameral* Congress.[[22]](#cite_note-25) Smaller states argued for equal representation for each state.[[23]](#cite_note-26) The two-chamber structure had functioned well in state governments.[[24]](#cite_note-27) A compromise plan, the [Connecticut Compromise](/wiki/Connecticut_Compromise "Connecticut Compromise"), was adopted with representatives chosen [by population](/wiki/Rep_by_pop "Rep by pop") (benefiting larger states) and exactly two senators chosen by state governments (benefiting smaller states).[[14]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14cc-17)[[25]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14dd5-28)

The ratified constitution created a [federal structure](/wiki/Federalism "Federalism") with two overlapping power centers so that each citizen *as an individual* is subject to the powers of state government and national government.[[26]](#cite_note-29)[[27]](#cite_note-30)[[28]](#cite_note-31) To protect against abuse of power, each branch of government – executive, legislative, and judicial – had a separate sphere of authority and could check other branches according to the principle of the [separation of powers](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers").[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11) Furthermore, there were checks and balances *within* the legislature since there were two separate chambers.[[29]](#cite_note-32) The new government became active in 1789.[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11)[[30]](#cite_note-33)

Political scientist [Julian E. Zelizer](/wiki/Julian_E._Zelizer "Julian E. Zelizer") suggested there were four main congressional eras, with considerable overlap, and included the *formative era* (1780s–1820s), the *partisan era* (1830s–1900s), the *committee era* (1910s–1960s), and the *contemporary era* (1970–present).[[31]](#cite_note-34)

[Federalists](/wiki/Federalist_Party "Federalist Party") and [anti-federalists](/wiki/Anti-Federalism "Anti-Federalism") jostled for power in the early years as political parties became pronounced. With the passage of the Constitution and the [Bill of Rights](/wiki/United_States_Bill_of_Rights "United States Bill of Rights"), the anti-federalist movement was exhausted. Some activists joined the [Anti-Administration Party](/wiki/Anti-Administration_Party "Anti-Administration Party") that [James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison") and [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") were forming about 1790–1791 to oppose policies of Treasury Secretary [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton"); it soon became the [Democratic-Republican Party](/wiki/Democratic-Republican_Party "Democratic-Republican Party") or the Jeffersonian Republican Party[[32]](#cite_note-35)[[33]](#cite_note-36) and thus began the era of the [First Party System](/wiki/First_Party_System "First Party System").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 19th century")]

In 1800, [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson")'s election to the presidency marked a [peaceful transition of power](/wiki/Peaceful_transition_of_power "Peaceful transition of power") between the parties. [John Marshall](/wiki/John_Marshall "John Marshall"), 4th chief justice of the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States"), empowered the courts by establishing the principle of [judicial review in law](/wiki/Judicial_review_in_the_United_States "Judicial review in the United States") in the landmark case *[Marbury v. Madison](/wiki/Marbury_v._Madison "Marbury v. Madison")* in 1803, effectively giving the Supreme Court a power to nullify congressional legislation.[[34]](#cite_note-37)[[35]](#cite_note-38)

The [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), which lasted from 1861 to 1865, resolved the slavery issue and unified the nation under federal authority but weakened the power of [states' rights](/wiki/States_rights "States rights"). The [Gilded Age](/wiki/Gilded_Age "Gilded Age") (1877–1901) was marked by [Republican](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_Republican_Party "History of the United States Republican Party") dominance of Congress. During this time, lobbying activity became more intense, particularly during the administration of President [Ulysses S. Grant](/wiki/Ulysses_S._Grant "Ulysses S. Grant") in which influential lobbies advocated for railroad subsidies and tariffs on wool.[[36]](#cite_note-39) Immigration and high birth rates swelled the ranks of citizens and the nation grew at a rapid pace. The [Progressive Era](/wiki/Progressive_Era "Progressive Era") was characterized by strong party leadership in both houses of Congress and calls for reform; sometimes reformers said lobbyists corrupted politics.[[37]](#cite_note-40) The position of [Speaker of the House](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_United_States_House_of_Representatives "Speaker of the United States House of Representatives") became extremely powerful under leaders such as [Thomas Reed](/wiki/Thomas_Brackett_Reed "Thomas Brackett Reed") in 1890 and [Joseph Gurney Cannon](/wiki/Joseph_Gurney_Cannon "Joseph Gurney Cannon").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 20th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/United_States_Congress_circa_1915.jpeg/250px-United_States_Congress_circa_1915.jpeg)](/wiki/File:United_States_Congress_circa_1915.jpeg)

The United States Congress, c. 1915

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Combined--Control_of_the_U.S._House_of_Representatives_-_Control_of_the_U.S._Senate.png/250px-Combined--Control_of_the_U.S._House_of_Representatives_-_Control_of_the_U.S._Senate.png)](/wiki/File:Combined--Control_of_the_U.S._House_of_Representatives_-_Control_of_the_U.S._Senate.png)

[Party control](/wiki/Party_divisions_of_United_States_Congresses "Party divisions of United States Congresses") of the Senate, House, and Presidency, 1855 to 2025.[[38]](#cite_note-41)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/1989_Congress_Bicentennial-Dollar.jpg/250px-1989_Congress_Bicentennial-Dollar.jpg)](/wiki/File:1989_Congress_Bicentennial-Dollar.jpg)

The 200th anniversary of Congress anniversary in 1989, which was honored by [United States Congress Bicentennial commemorative coins](/wiki/United_States_Congress_Bicentennial_commemorative_coins "United States Congress Bicentennial commemorative coins")

By the beginning of the 20th century, party structures and leadership emerged as key organizers of Senate proceedings.[[39]](#cite_note-42)

A system of seniority, in which long-time members of Congress gained more and more power, encouraged politicians of both parties to seek long terms. [Committee](/wiki/United_States_congressional_committees "United States congressional committees") chairmen remained influential in both houses until the reforms of the 1970s.[[40]](#cite_note-43)

Important structural changes included the [direct popular election](/wiki/Direct_popular_election "Direct popular election") of senators according to the [Seventeenth Amendment](/wiki/Seventeenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Seventeenth Amendment to the United States Constitution"),[[25]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14dd5-28) ratified on April 8, 1913. [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") decisions based on the Constitution's [commerce clause](/wiki/Commerce_clause "Commerce clause") expanded congressional power to regulate the economy.[[41]](#cite_note-44) One effect of popular election of senators was to reduce the difference between the House and Senate in terms of their link to the electorate.[[42]](#cite_note-45) [Lame duck](/wiki/Lame_duck_(politics) "Lame duck (politics)") reforms according to the [Twentieth Amendment](/wiki/Twentieth_amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Twentieth amendment to the United States Constitution") reduced the power of defeated and retiring members of Congress to wield influence despite their lack of accountability.[[43]](#cite_note-46)

The [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") ushered in President [Franklin Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_Roosevelt "Franklin Roosevelt") and strong control by Democrats[[44]](#cite_note-47) and historic [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") policies. [Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt")'s election in 1932 marked a shift in government power towards the executive branch. Numerous New Deal initiatives came from the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") rather initiated by Congress.[[45]](#cite_note-English14-48) President Roosevelt pushed his agenda in Congress by detailing Executive Branch staff to friendly Senate committees, a practice that ended with the Legislative Reorganization Act of 1946.[[46]](#cite_note-49)

The [Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") controlled both houses of Congress for many years.[[47]](#cite_note-50)[[48]](#cite_note-51)[[49]](#cite_note-1930lop-52) During this time, Republicans and conservative southern Democrats[[50]](#cite_note-53) formed the [Conservative Coalition](/wiki/Conservative_Coalition "Conservative Coalition").[[49]](#cite_note-1930lop-52)[[51]](#cite_note-54) Democrats maintained control of Congress during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[52]](#cite_note-55)[[53]](#cite_note-56) Congress struggled with efficiency in the postwar era partly by reducing the number of standing congressional committees.[[54]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11pp-57)

Southern Democrats became a powerful force in many influential committees although political power alternated between Republicans and Democrats during these years. More complex issues required greater specialization and expertise, such as space flight and atomic energy policy.[[54]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11pp-57) Senator [Joseph McCarthy](/wiki/Joseph_McCarthy "Joseph McCarthy") exploited the fear of communism during the [Second Red Scare](/wiki/McCarthyism "McCarthyism") and conducted televised hearings.[[55]](#cite_note-58)[[56]](#cite_note-59) In 1960, Democratic candidate [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy") narrowly won the presidency and power shifted again to the Democrats who dominated both chambers of Congress from 1961 to 1980, and retained a consistent majority in the House from 1955 to 1994.[[57]](#cite_note-60)

Congress enacted Johnson's [Great Society](/wiki/Great_Society "Great Society") program to fight poverty and hunger. The [Watergate Scandal](/wiki/Watergate_Scandal "Watergate Scandal") had a powerful effect of waking up a somewhat dormant Congress which investigated presidential wrongdoing and coverups; the scandal "substantially reshaped" relations between the branches of government, suggested political scientist [Bruce J. Schulman](/wiki/Bruce_J._Schulman "Bruce J. Schulman").[[58]](#cite_note-61) Partisanship returned, particularly after 1994; one analyst attributes partisan infighting to slim congressional majorities which discouraged friendly social gatherings in meeting rooms such as the *Board of Education*.[[13]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14dd4-16) Congress began reasserting its authority.[[45]](#cite_note-English14-48)[[59]](#cite_note-62)

[Lobbying](/wiki/Lobbying_in_the_United_States "Lobbying in the United States") became a big factor despite the 1971 [Federal Election Campaign Act](/wiki/Federal_Election_Campaign_Act "Federal Election Campaign Act"). [Political action committees](/wiki/Political_action_committee "Political action committee") or PACs could make substantive donations to congressional candidates via such means as *[soft money](/wiki/Soft_Money "Soft Money")* contributions.[[60]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11jggha-63) While soft money funds were not given to specific campaigns for candidates, the money often benefited candidates substantially in an indirect way and helped reelect candidates.[[60]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11jggha-63) Reforms such as the 2002 [Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act](/wiki/Bipartisan_Campaign_Reform_Act "Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act") limited campaign donations but did not limit *soft money* contributions.[[61]](#cite_note-64) One source suggests post-Watergate laws amended in 1974 meant to reduce the "influence of wealthy contributors and end payoffs" instead "legitimized PACs" since they "enabled individuals to band together in support of candidates".[[62]](#cite_note-tws02oct201-65)

From 1974 to 1984, PACs grew from 608 to 3,803 and donations leaped from $12.5 million to $120 million[[62]](#cite_note-tws02oct201-65)[[63]](#cite_note-tws02oct223-66)[[64]](#cite_note-tws01oct22-67) along with concern over PAC influence in Congress.[[65]](#cite_note-tws02oct205-68)[[66]](#cite_note-69) In 2009, there were 4,600 business, labor and special-interest PACs[[67]](#cite_note-tws02oct209-70) including ones for [lawyers](/wiki/Association_of_Trial_Lawyers_of_America "Association of Trial Lawyers of America"), [electricians](/wiki/International_Brotherhood_of_Electrical_Workers "International Brotherhood of Electrical Workers"), and [real estate brokers](/wiki/National_Association_of_Realtors "National Association of Realtors").[[68]](#cite_note-71) From 2007 to 2008, 175 members of Congress received "half or more of their campaign cash" from PACs.[[67]](#cite_note-tws02oct209-70)[[69]](#cite_note-72)[[70]](#cite_note-73)

From 1970 to 2009, the House expanded delegates, along with their powers and privileges representing U.S. citizens in non-state areas, beginning with representation on committees for [Puerto Rico's resident commissioner](/wiki/Resident_Commissioner_of_Puerto_Rico "Resident Commissioner of Puerto Rico") in 1970. In 1971, a delegate for the District of Columbia was authorized, and in 1972 new delegate positions were established for [U.S. Virgin Islands](/wiki/United_States_Virgin_Islands%27s_at-large_congressional_district "United States Virgin Islands's at-large congressional district") and [Guam](/wiki/Guam%27s_at-large_congressional_district "Guam's at-large congressional district"). In 1978, an additional delegate for [American Samoa](/wiki/American_Samoa%27s_at-large_congressional_district "American Samoa's at-large congressional district") were added.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the late 20th century, the media became more important in Congress's work.[[71]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14dd1-74) Analyst [Michael Schudson](/wiki/Michael_Schudson "Michael Schudson") suggested that greater publicity undermined the power of political parties and caused "more roads to open up in Congress for individual representatives to influence decisions".[[71]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14dd1-74) [Norman Ornstein](/wiki/Norman_Ornstein "Norman Ornstein") suggested that media prominence led to a greater emphasis on the negative and sensational side of Congress, and referred to this as the *[tabloidization](/wiki/Tabloidization "Tabloidization")* of media coverage.[[14]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14cc-17) Others saw pressure to squeeze a political position into a thirty-second soundbite.[[72]](#cite_note-75) A report characterized Congress in 2013 as unproductive, gridlocked, and "setting records for futility".[[73]](#cite_note-76)

In October 2013, with Congress unable to compromise, the government was shut down for several weeks and risked a serious default on debt payments, causing 60% of the public to say they would "fire every member of Congress" including their own representative.[[74]](#cite_note-77) One report suggested Congress posed the "biggest risk to the U.S. economy" because of its [brinksmanship](/wiki/Brinkmanship "Brinkmanship"), "down-to-the-wire budget and debt crises" and "indiscriminate spending cuts", resulting in slowed economic activity and keeping up to two million people unemployed.[[75]](#cite_note-78) There has been increasing public dissatisfaction with Congress,[[76]](#cite_note-79) with extremely low approval ratings[[77]](#cite_note-blogs.wsj.com-80)[[78]](#cite_note-firstread.nbcnews.com-81) which dropped to 5% in October 2013.[[79]](#cite_note-voa5congress-82)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 21st century")]

Further information: [Democratic backsliding in the United States § Congress](/wiki/Democratic_backsliding_in_the_United_States#Congress "Democratic backsliding in the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/2017-_Threats_against_members_of_Congress_and_their_families.svg/250px-2017-_Threats_against_members_of_Congress_and_their_families.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2017-_Threats_against_members_of_Congress_and_their_families.svg)

Each year, the USCP investigates thousands of "concerning statements" and direct threats against Members of Congress, their families, and staff. The number of such cases has grown in recent years, and tends to increase in election years.[[80]](#cite_note-USCP_20260127-83)

In 2009, Congress authorized another delegate for the [Northern Mariana Islands](/wiki/United_States_congressional_delegations_from_the_Northern_Mariana_Islands "United States congressional delegations from the Northern Mariana Islands"). These six members of Congress enjoy [floor privileges](/wiki/Floor_privileges_of_the_united_states_senate "Floor privileges of the united states senate") to introduce bills and resolutions, and in recent Congresses they vote in permanent and select committees, in party caucuses and in joint conferences with the Senate. They have Capitol Hill offices, staff and two annual appointments to each of the four military academies. While their votes are constitutional when Congress authorizes their House [Committee of the Whole](/wiki/Committee_of_the_Whole_(United_States_House_of_Representatives) "Committee of the Whole (United States House of Representatives)") votes, recent Congresses have not allowed for that, and they cannot vote when the House is meeting as the House of Representatives.[[81]](#cite_note-84)[[82]](#cite_note-85)

On January 6, 2021, Congress gathered to confirm the election of Joe Biden, when supporters of the outgoing president Donald Trump [attacked the building](/wiki/January_6_United_States_Capitol_attack "January 6 United States Capitol attack"). The session of Congress ended prematurely, and Congress representatives evacuated. [Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") supporters occupied Congress until D.C. police evacuated the area. The event was the first time since the [Burning of Washington](/wiki/Burning_of_Washington "Burning of Washington") by the British during the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812") that the United States Congress was forcefully occupied.[[83]](#cite_note-86)

## Women in Congress

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Women in Congress")]

Various social and structural barriers have prevented women from gaining seats in Congress. In the early 20th century, [women's domestic roles](/wiki/Cult_of_domesticity "Cult of domesticity") and the [inability to vote](/wiki/Disfranchisement "Disfranchisement") forestalled opportunities to run for and hold public office. The two party system and the lack of term limits favored incumbent white men, making the [widow's succession](/wiki/Widow%27s_succession "Widow's succession") – in which a woman temporarily took over a seat vacated by the death of her husband – the most common path to Congress for white women.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESanbonmatsu202042–43-87)

Women candidates began making substantial inroads in the later 20th century, due in part to new political support mechanisms and public awareness of their underrepresentation in Congress.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESanbonmatsu202045-88) Recruitment and [financial support](/wiki/Campaign_finance "Campaign finance") for women candidates were rare until the [second-wave feminism movement](/wiki/Second-wave_feminism "Second-wave feminism"), when [activists](/wiki/Activism "Activism") moved into electoral politics. Beginning in the 1970s, donors and [political action committees](/wiki/Political_action_committee "Political action committee") like [EMILY's List](/wiki/EMILY%27s_List "EMILY's List") began recruiting, training and funding women candidates. Watershed political moments like the [confirmation of Clarence Thomas](/wiki/Clarence_Thomas_Supreme_Court_nomination "Clarence Thomas Supreme Court nomination") and the [2016 presidential election](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election "2016 United States presidential election") created momentum for women candidates, resulting in the [Year of the Woman](/wiki/Year_of_the_Woman "Year of the Woman") and the election of members of [The Squad](/wiki/The_Squad_(United_States_Congress) "The Squad (United States Congress)"), respectively.[[86]](#cite_note-89)[[87]](#cite_note-90)

Women of color faced additional challenges that made their ascension to Congress even more difficult. [Jim Crow laws](/wiki/Jim_Crow_laws "Jim Crow laws"), [voter suppression](/wiki/Voter_suppression_in_the_United_States "Voter suppression in the United States") and other forms of structural racism made it virtually impossible for women of color to reach Congress prior to 1965. The passage of the [Voting Rights Act that year](/wiki/Voting_Rights_Act_of_1965 "Voting Rights Act of 1965"), and the elimination of race-based immigration laws in the 1960s opened the possibility for Black, Asian American, Latina and other non-white women candidates to run for Congress.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESanbonmatsu202044–45-91)

Racially polarized voting, racial stereotypes and lack of institutional support still prevent women of color from reaching Congress as easily as [white people](/wiki/White_people "White people"). Senate elections, which require victories in statewide electorates, have been particularly difficult for women of color.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESanbonmatsu202042-92) [Carol Moseley Braun](/wiki/Carol_Moseley_Braun "Carol Moseley Braun") became the first woman of color to reach the Senate in 1993. The second, [Mazie Hirono](/wiki/Mazie_Hirono "Mazie Hirono"), won in 2013.

In 2021, [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris") became the first female [President of the Senate](/wiki/President_of_the_Senate "President of the Senate"), which came with her role as the first female [Vice President of the United States](/wiki/Vice_President_of_the_United_States "Vice President of the United States").[[90]](#cite_note-93)

## Role

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Role")]

### Powers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Powers")]

Main article: [Powers of the United States Congress](/wiki/Powers_of_the_United_States_Congress "Powers of the United States Congress")

#### Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Overview")]

[![$100,000-dollar bill.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/USCurrency_Federal_Reserve.jpg/250px-USCurrency_Federal_Reserve.jpg)](/wiki/File:USCurrency_Federal_Reserve.jpg)

Congress's [power of the purse](/wiki/Power_of_the_purse "Power of the purse"), which authorizes it to tax citizens, spend money, and print currency

[![Aircraft carrier at sea.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/USS_Bon_Homme_Richard_%28CVA-31%29_underway_in_the_Gulf_of_Tonkin_on_2_November_1964.jpg/250px-USS_Bon_Homme_Richard_%28CVA-31%29_underway_in_the_Gulf_of_Tonkin_on_2_November_1964.jpg)](/wiki/File:USS_Bon_Homme_Richard_(CVA-31)_underway_in_the_Gulf_of_Tonkin_on_2_November_1964.jpg)

Congress authorizes defense spending, such as the purchase of the [USS *Bon Homme Richard* (CV-31)](/wiki/USS_Bon_Homme_Richard_(CV-31) "USS Bon Homme Richard (CV-31)")

[![Seated suits behind a microphone.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/ThompsonWatergate.jpg/250px-ThompsonWatergate.jpg)](/wiki/File:ThompsonWatergate.jpg)

The [Senate Watergate Committee](/wiki/Senate_Watergate_Committee "Senate Watergate Committee"), investigating President [Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") and the [Watergate scandal](/wiki/Watergate_scandal "Watergate scandal") from 1973 to 1974

[Article One of the Constitution](/wiki/Article_One_of_the_United_States_Constitution "Article One of the United States Constitution") creates and sets forth the structure and most of the powers of Congress. Sections One through Six describe how Congress is elected and gives each House the power to create its own structure. Section Seven lays out the process for creating laws, and Section Eight enumerates numerous powers. Section Nine is a list of powers Congress does not have, and Section Ten enumerates powers of the state, some of which may only be granted by Congress.[[91]](#cite_note-94) [Constitutional amendments](/wiki/Constitutional_amendment "Constitutional amendment") have granted Congress additional powers. Congress also has [implied powers](/wiki/Implied_powers "Implied powers") derived from the Constitution's [Necessary and Proper Clause](/wiki/Necessary_and_Proper_Clause "Necessary and Proper Clause").[[92]](#cite_note-95)

Congress has authority over financial and budgetary policy through the enumerated power to "lay and collect Taxes, Duties, Imposts and Excises, to pay the Debts and provide for the common Defence and general Welfare of the United States". There is vast authority over budgets, although analyst Eric Patashnik suggested that much of Congress's power to manage the budget has been lost when the welfare state expanded since "entitlements were institutionally detached from Congress's ordinary legislative routine and rhythm."[[93]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14dd2-96) Another factor leading to less control over the budget was a [Keynesian](/wiki/Keynesian_economics "Keynesian economics") belief that balanced budgets were unnecessary.[[93]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14dd2-96)

The [Sixteenth Amendment](/wiki/Sixteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Sixteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") in 1913 extended congressional power of taxation to include [income taxes](/wiki/Income_tax "Income tax") without apportionment among the several States, and without regard to any census or enumeration.[[94]](#cite_note-davidson-p18-97) The Constitution also grants Congress the exclusive power to appropriate funds, and this *[power of the purse](/wiki/Power_of_the_purse "Power of the purse")* is one of Congress's primary [checks](/wiki/Separation_of_powers_under_the_United_States_Constitution#Checks_and_balances "Separation of powers under the United States Constitution") on the executive branch.[[94]](#cite_note-davidson-p18-97) Congress can borrow money on the credit of the United States, [regulate commerce](/wiki/Commerce_Clause "Commerce Clause") with foreign nations and among the states, and coin money.[[95]](#cite_note-98) Generally, the Senate and the House of Representatives have equal legislative authority, although only the House may originate revenue and [appropriation bills](/wiki/Appropriations_bill_(United_States) "Appropriations bill (United States)").[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11)

Congress has an important role in [national defense](/wiki/Defense_(military) "Defense (military)"), including the exclusive power to declare war, to raise and maintain the [armed forces](/wiki/Military_of_the_United_States "Military of the United States"), and to make rules for the military.[[96]](#cite_note-99) Some critics charge that the [executive branch](/wiki/Executive_branch "Executive branch") has usurped Congress's constitutionally defined task of declaring war.[[97]](#cite_note-100) While historically presidents initiated the process for going to war, they asked for and received formal war declarations from Congress for the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), the [Mexican–American War](/wiki/Mexican%E2%80%93American_War "Mexican–American War"), the [Spanish–American War](/wiki/Spanish%E2%80%93American_War "Spanish–American War"), [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), and [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"),[[98]](#cite_note-tws28sep07-101) although President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt")'s military move into Panama in 1903 did not get congressional approval.[[98]](#cite_note-tws28sep07-101) In the early days after the [North Korean invasion of 1950](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War"), President [Truman](/wiki/Harry_S._Truman "Harry S. Truman") described the American response as a "police action".[[99]](#cite_note-102) According to *[Time](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)")* magazine in 1970, "U.S. presidents [had] ordered troops into position or action without a formal congressional declaration a total of 149 times."[[98]](#cite_note-tws28sep07-101) In 1993, [Michael Kinsley](/wiki/Michael_Kinsley "Michael Kinsley") wrote that "Congress's war power has become the most flagrantly disregarded provision in the Constitution," and that the "real erosion [of Congress's war power] began after World War II."[[100]](#cite_note-103)[[101]](#cite_note-104)[[102]](#cite_note-105) Disagreement about the extent of congressional versus presidential power regarding war has been present periodically throughout the nation's history.[[103]](#cite_note-106)

Congress can establish post offices and post roads, issue patents and [copyrights](/wiki/Copyright "Copyright"), fix standards of weights and measures, establish [Courts inferior to the Supreme Court](/wiki/Inferior_courts_of_the_United_States "Inferior courts of the United States"), and "make all Laws which shall be necessary and proper for carrying into Execution the foregoing Powers, and all other Powers vested by this Constitution in the Government of the United States, or in any Department or Officer thereof". [Article Four](/wiki/Article_Four_of_the_United_States_Constitution "Article Four of the United States Constitution") gives Congress the power to admit new states into the Union.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

One of Congress's foremost non-legislative functions is the power to [investigate](/wiki/Congressional_investigation "Congressional investigation") and oversee the executive branch.[[104]](#cite_note-107) [Congressional oversight](/wiki/Congressional_oversight "Congressional oversight") is usually delegated to [committees](/wiki/United_States_congressional_committee "United States congressional committee") and is facilitated by Congress's subpoena power.[[105]](#cite_note-108) Some critics have charged that Congress has in some instances failed to do an adequate job of [overseeing](/wiki/Congressional_oversight "Congressional oversight") the other branches of government. In the [Plame affair](/wiki/Plame_affair "Plame affair"), critics including Representative [Henry A. Waxman](/wiki/Henry_A._Waxman "Henry A. Waxman") charged that Congress was not doing an adequate job of oversight in this case.[[106]](#cite_note-109) There have been concerns about congressional oversight of executive actions such as [warrantless wiretapping](/wiki/Warrantless_wiretapping "Warrantless wiretapping"), although others respond that Congress did investigate the legality of presidential decisions.[[107]](#cite_note-110) Political scientists Ornstein and Mann suggested that oversight functions do not help members of Congress win reelection. Congress also has the exclusive [power of removal](/wiki/Federal_impeachment_in_the_United_States "Federal impeachment in the United States"), allowing impeachment and removal of the president, federal judges and other federal officers.[[108]](#cite_note-111) There have been charges that presidents acting under the doctrine of the [unitary executive](/wiki/Unitary_executive "Unitary executive") have assumed important legislative and budgetary powers that should belong to Congress.[[109]](#cite_note-112) So-called [signing statements](/wiki/Signing_statements "Signing statements") are one way in which a president can "tip the balance of power between Congress and the White House a little more in favor of the executive branch", according to one account.[[110]](#cite_note-113) Past presidents, including [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan"), [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush"), [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton"), and [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush"),[[111]](#cite_note-114) have made public statements when signing congressional legislation about how they understand a bill or plan to execute it, and commentators, including the [American Bar Association](/wiki/American_Bar_Association "American Bar Association"), have described this practice as against the spirit of the Constitution.[[112]](#cite_note-115)[[113]](#cite_note-116) There have been concerns that presidential authority to cope with financial crises is eclipsing the power of Congress.[[114]](#cite_note-117) In 2008, [George F. Will](/wiki/George_F._Will "George F. Will") called the Capitol building a "tomb for the antiquated idea that the legislative branch matters".[[115]](#cite_note-tws28sep-118)

#### Enumeration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Enumeration")]

The Constitution enumerates the powers of Congress in detail. In addition, other congressional powers have been granted, or confirmed, by constitutional amendments. The [Thirteenth](/wiki/Thirteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Thirteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") (1865), [Fourteenth](/wiki/Fourteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Fourteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") (1868), and [Fifteenth Amendments](/wiki/Fifteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Fifteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution") (1870) gave Congress authority to enact legislation to enforce rights of African Americans, including [voting rights](/wiki/Voting_rights "Voting rights"), [due process](/wiki/Due_process "Due process"), and [equal protection](/wiki/Equal_protection "Equal protection") under the law.[[116]](#cite_note-119) Generally militia forces are controlled by state governments, not Congress.[[117]](#cite_note-120)

#### Implicit, commerce clause

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Implicit, commerce clause")]

Congress also has [implied powers](/wiki/Implied_powers "Implied powers") deriving from the Constitution's [Necessary and Proper Clause](/wiki/Necessary_and_Proper_Clause "Necessary and Proper Clause") which permit Congress to "make all laws which shall be necessary and proper for carrying into Execution the foregoing Powers, and all other Powers vested by this Constitution in the Government of the United States, or in any Department or Officer thereof".[[118]](#cite_note-121) Broad interpretations of this clause and of the [Commerce Clause](/wiki/Commerce_Clause "Commerce Clause"), the enumerated power to regulate commerce, in rulings such as [*McCulloch v. Maryland*](/wiki/McCulloch_v_Maryland "McCulloch v Maryland"), have effectively widened the scope of Congress's legislative authority far beyond that prescribed in Section Eight.[[119]](#cite_note-122)[[120]](#cite_note-123)

#### Territorial government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Territorial government")]

Main articles: [Territories of the United States](/wiki/Territories_of_the_United_States "Territories of the United States") and [Non-voting members of the United States House of Representatives](/wiki/Non-voting_members_of_the_United_States_House_of_Representatives "Non-voting members of the United States House of Representatives")

Constitutional responsibility for the oversight of [Washington, D.C.](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C."), the federal district and national capital, and the U.S. territories of [Guam](/wiki/Guam "Guam"), [American Samoa](/wiki/American_Samoa "American Samoa"), [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico"), the [U.S. Virgin Islands](/wiki/U.S._Virgin_Islands "U.S. Virgin Islands"), and the [Northern Mariana Islands](/wiki/Northern_Mariana_Islands "Northern Mariana Islands") rests with Congress.[[121]](#cite_note-124) The republican form of government in territories is devolved by congressional statute to the respective territories including direct election of governors, the D.C. mayor and locally elective territorial legislatures.[[122]](#cite_note-125)

Each territory and Washington, D.C., elects a non-voting delegate to the U.S. House of Representatives as they have throughout congressional history. They "possess the same powers as other members of the House, except that they may not vote when the House is meeting as the House of Representatives". They are assigned offices and allowances for staff, participate in debate, and appoint constituents to the four military service academies for the Army, Navy, Air Force and Coast Guard.[[123]](#cite_note-126)

Washington, D.C., citizens alone among U.S. territories have the right to directly vote for the President of the United States, although the Democratic and Republican political parties nominate their presidential candidates at national conventions which include delegates from the five major territories.[[124]](#cite_note-127)

### Checks and balances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Checks and balances")]

Main article: [U.S. Congress in relation to the president and Supreme Court](/wiki/U.S._Congress_in_relation_to_the_president_and_Supreme_Court "U.S. Congress in relation to the president and Supreme Court")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/United_States_Capitol_seen_from_the_United_States_Supreme_Court%2C_Washington%2C_DC_-_20080326.jpg/250px-United_States_Capitol_seen_from_the_United_States_Supreme_Court%2C_Washington%2C_DC_-_20080326.jpg)](/wiki/File:United_States_Capitol_seen_from_the_United_States_Supreme_Court,_Washington,_DC_-_20080326.jpg)

The [United States Capitol](/wiki/United_States_Capitol "United States Capitol") seen from the [United States Supreme Court building](/wiki/United_States_Supreme_Court_building "United States Supreme Court building")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Senate_in_session.jpg/250px-Senate_in_session.jpg)](/wiki/File:Senate_in_session.jpg)

The [impeachment trial of President Clinton](/wiki/Impeachment_trial_of_Bill_Clinton "Impeachment trial of Bill Clinton") in 1999, presided over by [Chief Justice William Rehnquist](/wiki/William_H._Rehnquist "William H. Rehnquist")

Representative [Lee H. Hamilton](/wiki/Lee_H._Hamilton "Lee H. Hamilton") explained how Congress functions within the federal government:

> To me the key to understanding it is balance. The founders went to great lengths to balance institutions against each other – balancing powers among the three branches: Congress, the president, and the Supreme Court; between the House of Representatives and the Senate; between the federal government and the states; among states of different sizes and regions with different interests; between the powers of government and the rights of citizens, as spelled out in the Bill of Rights ... No one part of government dominates the other.[[11]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14aa-14): 6

The Constitution provides [checks and balances](/wiki/Separation_of_powers_under_the_United_States_Constitution "Separation of powers under the United States Constitution") among the three branches of the federal government. Its authors expected the greater power to lie with Congress as described in Article One.[[11]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14aa-14)[[125]](#cite_note-128)

The influence of Congress on the presidency has varied from period to period depending on factors such as congressional leadership, presidential political influence, historical circumstances such as war, and individual initiative by members of Congress. [The impeachment](/wiki/Impeachment_of_Andrew_Johnson "Impeachment of Andrew Johnson") of [Andrew Johnson](/wiki/Andrew_Johnson "Andrew Johnson") made the presidency less powerful than Congress for a considerable period afterwards.[[126]](#cite_note-129) The 20th and 21st centuries have seen the rise of presidential power under politicians such as [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt"), [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson"), [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt"), [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon"), [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan"), and [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush").[[127]](#cite_note-kingwh-130) Congress restricted presidential power with laws such as the [Congressional Budget and Impoundment Control Act of 1974](/wiki/Congressional_Budget_and_Impoundment_Control_Act_of_1974 "Congressional Budget and Impoundment Control Act of 1974") and the [War Powers Resolution](/wiki/War_Powers_Resolution "War Powers Resolution"). The presidency remains considerably more powerful today than during the 19th century.[[11]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14aa-14)[[127]](#cite_note-kingwh-130) Executive branch officials are often loath to reveal sensitive information to members of Congress because of concern that information could not be kept secret; in return, knowing they may be in the dark about executive branch activity, congressional officials are more likely to distrust their counterparts in executive agencies.[[128]](#cite_note-131) Many government actions require fast coordinated effort by many agencies, and this is a task that Congress is ill-suited for. Congress is slow, open, divided, and not well matched to handle more rapid executive action or do a good job of overseeing such activity, according to one analysis.[[129]](#cite_note-132)

The Constitution concentrates removal powers in the Congress by empowering and obligating the House of Representatives to [impeach](/wiki/Federal_impeachment_in_the_United_States "Federal impeachment in the United States") executive or judicial officials for "Treason, Bribery, or other high Crimes and Misdemeanors". Impeachment is a formal accusation of unlawful activity by a civil officer or government official. The Senate is constitutionally empowered and obligated to try all impeachments. A simple majority in the House is required to impeach an official; a two-thirds majority in the Senate is required for conviction. A convicted official is automatically removed from office; in addition, the Senate may stipulate that the [defendant](/wiki/Defendant "Defendant") be banned from holding office in the future. Impeachment proceedings may not inflict more than this. A convicted party may face criminal penalties in a normal court of law. In the history of the United States, the House of Representatives has impeached sixteen officials, of whom seven were convicted. Another resigned before the Senate could complete the trial. Only three presidents have ever been impeached: [Andrew Johnson](/wiki/Andrew_Johnson "Andrew Johnson") in 1868, [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") in 1999, [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") in 2019 and 2021. The [trials](/wiki/Trial_(law) "Trial (law)") of Johnson, Clinton, and the 2019 trial of Trump all ended in acquittal; in Johnson's case, the Senate fell one vote short of the two-thirds majority required for [conviction](/wiki/Conviction_(law) "Conviction (law)"). In 1974, [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") resigned from office after [impeachment proceedings](/wiki/Impeachment_process_against_Richard_Nixon "Impeachment process against Richard Nixon") in the [House Judiciary Committee](/wiki/House_Judiciary_Committee "House Judiciary Committee") indicated his removal from office.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Senate has an important check on the executive power by confirming [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_the_United_States "Cabinet of the United States") officials, judges, and other high officers "by and with the Advice and Consent of the Senate". It confirms most presidential nominees, but rejections are not uncommon. Furthermore, treaties negotiated by the President must be ratified by a two-thirds majority vote in the Senate to take effect. As a result, presidential arm-twisting of senators can happen before a key vote; for example, President Obama's secretary of state, [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton"), urged her former senate colleagues to approve a nuclear arms treaty with Russia in 2010.[[130]](#cite_note-133) The House of Representatives has no formal role in either the ratification of treaties or the appointment of federal officials, other than in [filling a vacancy](/wiki/Twenty-fifth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution#Section_2:_Vice_presidential_vacancy "Twenty-fifth Amendment to the United States Constitution") in the office of the vice president; in such a case, a majority vote in each House is required to confirm a president's nomination of a vice president.[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11)

In 1803, the Supreme Court established [judicial review](/wiki/Judicial_review "Judicial review") of federal legislation in *[Marbury v. Madison](/wiki/Marbury_v._Madison "Marbury v. Madison")*, holding that Congress could not grant unconstitutional power to the Court itself. The Constitution did not explicitly state that the courts may exercise judicial review. The notion that courts could declare laws [unconstitutional](/wiki/Unconstitutional "Unconstitutional") was envisioned by the [founding fathers](/wiki/Founding_Fathers_of_the_United_States "Founding Fathers of the United States"). [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton"), for example, mentioned and expounded upon the doctrine in [Federalist No. 78](/wiki/Federalist_No._78 "Federalist No. 78"). [Originalists](/wiki/Originalism "Originalism") on the Supreme Court have argued that if the constitution does not say something explicitly it is unconstitutional to infer what it should, might, or could have said.[[131]](#cite_note-134) Judicial review means that the Supreme Court can nullify a congressional law. It is a huge check by the courts on the legislative authority and limits congressional power substantially. In 1857, for example, the Supreme Court struck down provisions of a congressional act of 1820 in its [Dred Scott](/wiki/Dred_Scott "Dred Scott") decision.[[132]](#cite_note-135) At the same time, the Supreme Court can extend congressional power through its constitutional interpretations.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The congressional inquiry into [St. Clair's Defeat](/wiki/St._Clair%27s_Defeat "St. Clair's Defeat") of 1791 was the first congressional investigation of the executive branch.[[133]](#cite_note-136) Investigations are conducted to gather information on the need for future legislation, to test the effectiveness of laws already passed, and to inquire into the qualifications and performance of members and officials of the other branches. Committees may hold hearings, and, if necessary, [subpoena](/wiki/Subpoena "Subpoena") people to testify when investigating issues over which it has the power to legislate.[[134]](#cite_note-137)[[135]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11dd-138) Witnesses who refuse to testify may be cited for [contempt of Congress](/wiki/Contempt_of_Congress "Contempt of Congress"), and those who testify falsely may be charged with [perjury](/wiki/Perjury "Perjury"). Most committee hearings are open to the public (the [House](/wiki/United_States_House_Permanent_Select_Committee_on_Intelligence "United States House Permanent Select Committee on Intelligence") and [Senate intelligence committees](/wiki/United_States_Senate_Select_Committee_on_Intelligence "United States Senate Select Committee on Intelligence") are the exception); important hearings are widely reported in the mass media and transcripts published a few months afterwards.[[135]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11dd-138) Congress, in the course of studying possible laws and investigating matters, generates an incredible amount of information in various forms, and can be described as a publisher.[[136]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11ff-139) Indeed, it publishes House and Senate reports[[136]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11ff-139) and maintains databases which are updated irregularly with publications in a variety of electronic formats.[[136]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11ff-139)

Congress also plays a role in presidential elections. Both Houses meet in joint session on the sixth day of January following a presidential election to count the electoral votes, and there are procedures to follow if no candidate wins a majority.[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11)

The main result of congressional activity is the creation of laws,[[137]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11cc-140) most of which are contained in the United States Code, arranged by subject matter alphabetically under fifty title headings to present the laws "in a concise and usable form".[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11)

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Structure")]

Main article: [Structure of the United States Congress](/wiki/Structure_of_the_United_States_Congress "Structure of the United States Congress")

Congress is split into two chambers – House and Senate – and manages the task of writing national legislation by dividing work into separate committees which specialize in different areas. Some members of Congress are elected by their peers to be officers of these committees. Congress has ancillary organizations such as the [Government Accountability Office](/wiki/Government_Accountability_Office "Government Accountability Office") and the [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress") to help provide it with information, and members of Congress have staff and offices to assist them as well. In addition, a vast industry of lobbyists helps members write legislation on behalf of diverse corporate and labor interests.

### Committees

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Committees")]

Main article: [United States congressional committee](/wiki/United_States_congressional_committee "United States congressional committee")

[![Photo of a table with chairs.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/Congress_Hall_committee_room_2.jpg/250px-Congress_Hall_committee_room_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Congress_Hall_committee_room_2.jpg)

The second committee room at [Congress Hall](/wiki/Congress_Hall "Congress Hall") in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia")



A [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress") video explaining the committees of the United States Congress

#### Specializations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Specializations")]

The committee structure permits members of Congress to study a particular subject intensely. It is neither expected nor possible that a member be an expert on all subject areas before Congress.[[138]](#cite_note-141) As time goes by, members develop expertise in particular subjects and their legal aspects. [Committees](/wiki/United_States_congressional_committee "United States congressional committee") investigate specialized subjects and advise the entire Congress about choices and trade-offs. The choice of specialty may be influenced by the member's constituency, important regional issues, prior background and experience.[[139]](#cite_note-142) Senators often choose a different specialty from that of the other senator from their state to prevent overlap.[[140]](#cite_note-143) Some committees specialize in running the business of other committees and exert a powerful influence over all legislation; for example, the [House Ways and Means Committee](/wiki/House_Ways_and_Means_Committee "House Ways and Means Committee") has considerable influence over House affairs.[[141]](#cite_note-144)

#### Power

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Power")]

Committees write legislation. While procedures, such as the House [discharge petition](/wiki/Discharge_petition "Discharge petition") process, can introduce bills to the House floor and effectively bypass committee input, they are exceedingly difficult to implement without committee action. Committees have power and have been called *independent fiefdoms*. Legislative, oversight, and internal administrative tasks are divided among about two hundred committees and [subcommittees](/wiki/United_States_congressional_subcommittee "United States congressional subcommittee") which gather information, evaluate alternatives, and identify problems.[[142]](#cite_note-House_Rules-145) They propose solutions for consideration by the full chamber.[[142]](#cite_note-House_Rules-145) In addition, they perform the function of *oversight* by monitoring the executive branch and investigating wrongdoing.[[142]](#cite_note-House_Rules-145)

#### Officer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Officer")]

At the start of each two-year session, the House elects a [speaker](/wiki/Speaker_of_the_United_States_House_of_Representatives "Speaker of the United States House of Representatives") who does not normally preside over debates but serves as the majority party's leader. In the Senate, the vice president is the *[ex officio](/wiki/Ex_officio "Ex officio")* president of the Senate. In addition, the Senate elects an officer called the [president pro tempore](/wiki/President_pro_tempore_of_the_United_States_Senate "President pro tempore of the United States Senate"). *Pro tempore* means *for the time being*. This office is usually held by the most senior member of the Senate's majority party and customarily keeps this position until there is a change in party control. Accordingly, the Senate does not necessarily elect a new president pro tempore at the beginning of a new Congress. In the House and Senate, the actual presiding officer is generally a junior member of the majority party, appointed on a rotating basis.[[143]](#cite_note-146)[[144]](#cite_note-147)

### Support services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Support services")]

#### Library of Congress

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Library of Congress")]

Main article: [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Thomas_Jefferson_Building_Aerial_by_Carol_M._Highsmith.jpg/250px-Thomas_Jefferson_Building_Aerial_by_Carol_M._Highsmith.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Jefferson_Building_Aerial_by_Carol_M._Highsmith.jpg)

Library of Congress Jefferson Building

The [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress") (LOC) was established by an act of Congress in 1800. It is primarily housed in three buildings on [Capitol Hill](/wiki/Capitol_Hill "Capitol Hill"), but also includes several other sites: the [National Library Service for the Blind and Physically Handicapped](/wiki/National_Library_Service_for_the_Blind_and_Physically_Handicapped "National Library Service for the Blind and Physically Handicapped") in Washington, D.C.; the [National Audio-Visual Conservation Center](/wiki/National_Audio-Visual_Conservation_Center "National Audio-Visual Conservation Center") in [Culpeper, Virginia](/wiki/Culpeper,_Virginia "Culpeper, Virginia"); a large [book storage](/wiki/Book_storage "Book storage") facility located in [Fort Meade, Maryland](/wiki/Fort_Meade,_Maryland "Fort Meade, Maryland"); and multiple overseas offices. The Library had mostly law books when it [was burnt](/wiki/Burning_of_Washington "Burning of Washington") by [British](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire") forces in 1814 during the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812").[[145]](#cite_note-148)

The library's collections were restored and expanded when Congress authorized the purchase of [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson")'s private library. One of the library's missions is to serve Congress and its staff as well as the American public. It is the largest library in the world, with nearly 150 million items including books, films, maps, photographs, music, manuscripts, graphics, and materials in 470 languages.[[146]](#cite_note-149)

##### Congressional Research Service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Congressional Research Service")]

Main article: [Congressional Research Service](/wiki/Congressional_Research_Service "Congressional Research Service")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Congressional_Research_Service.svg/250px-Congressional_Research_Service.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Congressional_Research_Service.svg)

The [Congressional Research Service](/wiki/Congressional_Research_Service "Congressional Research Service")

The [Congressional Research Service](/wiki/Congressional_Research_Service "Congressional Research Service") (CRS), part of the Library of Congress, provides detailed, up-to-date and non-partisan research for senators, representatives, and their staff to help them carry out their official duties. It provides ideas for legislation, helps members analyze a bill, facilitates public hearings, makes reports, consults on matters such as parliamentary procedure, and helps the two chambers resolve disagreements. It has been called the "House's think tank" and has a staff of about 900 employees.[[147]](#cite_note-150)

#### Congressional Budget Office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Congressional Budget Office")]

Main article: [Congressional Budget Office](/wiki/Congressional_Budget_Office "Congressional Budget Office")

The [Congressional Budget Office](/wiki/Congressional_Budget_Office "Congressional Budget Office") (CBO) is a [federal agency](/wiki/List_of_United_States_federal_agencies "List of United States federal agencies") which provides [economic data](/wiki/Economics "Economics") to Congress.[[148]](#cite_note-151)

It was created as an independent non-partisan agency by the [Congressional Budget and Impoundment Control Act of 1974](/wiki/Congressional_Budget_and_Impoundment_Control_Act_of_1974 "Congressional Budget and Impoundment Control Act of 1974"). It helps Congress estimate revenue inflows from taxes and helps the budgeting process. It makes projections about such matters as the [national debt](/wiki/National_debt "National debt")[[149]](#cite_note-152) as well as likely costs of legislation. It prepares an annual *Economic and Budget Outlook* with a mid-year update and writes *An Analysis of the President's Budgetary Proposals* for the [Senate's Appropriations Committee](/wiki/Senate_Committee_on_Appropriations "Senate Committee on Appropriations"). The speaker of the House and the Senate's president pro tempore jointly appoint the CBO director for a four-year term.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Government Accountability Office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Government Accountability Office")]

Main articles: [Government Accountability Office](/wiki/Government_Accountability_Office "Government Accountability Office") and [United States Government Accountability Office](/wiki/United_States_Government_Accountability_Office "United States Government Accountability Office")

The [Government Accountability Office](/wiki/United_States_Government_Accountability_Office "United States Government Accountability Office") (GAO), is a [federal agency](/wiki/List_of_United_States_federal_agencies "List of United States federal agencies") within the legislative branch that provides [auditing](/wiki/Audit "Audit"), [evaluative](/wiki/Evaluation "Evaluation"), and investigative services for the United States Congress in an independent and nonpartisan capacity.[[150]](#cite_note-153)

The GAO is the [supreme audit institution](/wiki/Supreme_audit_institution "Supreme audit institution") of the [federal government of the United States](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States"). It identifies its core "mission values" as: accountability, integrity, and reliability.[[151]](#cite_note-154) It is also known as the "congressional watchdog".[[152]](#cite_note-155)

#### Architect of the Capitol

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Architect of the Capitol")]

Main article: [Architect of the Capitol](/wiki/Architect_of_the_Capitol "Architect of the Capitol")

The [Architect of the Capitol](/wiki/Architect_of_the_Capitol "Architect of the Capitol") (AOC) is a [federal agency](/wiki/List_of_United_States_federal_agencies "List of United States federal agencies") within the legislative branch that is responsible for the [maintenance](/wiki/Maintenance "Maintenance"), operation, development, [construction](/wiki/Construction "Construction"), [building preservation](/wiki/Historic_preservation "Historic preservation"), and [property management](/wiki/Property_management "Property management") of the [United States Capitol Complex](/wiki/United_States_Capitol_Complex "United States Capitol Complex")[[153]](#cite_note-156) and is accountable directly to the United States Congress and the [Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States").[[154]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-157)

#### United States Capitol Police

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: United States Capitol Police")]

Main article: [United States Capitol Police](/wiki/United_States_Capitol_Police "United States Capitol Police")

#### Lobbying, government relations, and advocacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Lobbying, government relations, and advocacy")]

Main articles: [Lobbying in the United States](/wiki/Lobbying_in_the_United_States "Lobbying in the United States"), [Lobbying](/wiki/Lobbying "Lobbying"), [Government relations](/wiki/Government_relations "Government relations"), [Policy advocacy](/wiki/Policy_advocacy "Policy advocacy"), and [Advocacy group](/wiki/Advocacy_group "Advocacy group")

[Lobbyists, advocates, principals, and constituents](/wiki/Lobbying#Types_of_practitioners "Lobbying") as well as others that operate in the field of government relations, represent diverse interests and often seek to influence congressional decisions to reflect their clients' or their own needs.
