Lobbing firms, government relations firms, [advocacy groups](/wiki/Advocacy_group "Advocacy group"), businesses, [nonprofits](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization"), other organizations and their members that conduct [lobbying](/wiki/Lobbying "Lobbying") or [policy advocacy](/wiki/Policy_advocacy "Policy advocacy") activities sometimes write legislation and [whip](/wiki/Whip_(politics) "Whip (politics)") bills. In 2007, there were approximately 17,000 federal lobbyists in Washington, D.C,[[155]](#cite_note-158) though not all members of the government relations industry are lobbyists requiring registry on an official [lobby register](/wiki/Lobby_register "Lobby register"). They explain to legislators the goals of their organizations. Some lobbyists represent non-profit organizations and some work [pro bono](/wiki/Pro_bono "Pro bono") for issues in which they are personally interested.[[d]](#cite_note-170)

### Partisanship versus bipartisanship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Partisanship versus bipartisanship")]

Congress has alternated between periods of constructive cooperation and compromise between parties, known as [bipartisanship](/wiki/Bipartisanship "Bipartisanship"), and periods of deep political [polarization](/wiki/Polarization_(politics) "Polarization (politics)") and fierce infighting, known as [partisanship](/wiki/Partisanship "Partisanship"). The period after the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") was marked by partisanship, as is the case today. It is generally easier for committees to reach accord on issues when compromise is possible. Some [political scientists](/wiki/Political_science "Political science") speculate that a prolonged period marked by narrow majorities in both chambers of Congress has intensified partisanship in the last few decades, but that an alternation of control of Congress between Democrats and Republicans may lead to greater flexibility in policies, as well as pragmatism and civility within the institution.[[167]](#cite_note-171)

## Procedures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Procedures")]

Main article: [Procedures of the United States Congress](/wiki/Procedures_of_the_United_States_Congress "Procedures of the United States Congress")

### Sessions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Sessions")]

A term of Congress is divided into two "[sessions](/wiki/Legislative_session "Legislative session")", one for each year; Congress has occasionally been called into an extra or *[special session](/wiki/Special_session "Special session")*. A new session commences on January 3 each year unless Congress decides differently. The Constitution requires Congress to meet at least once each year and forbids either house from meeting outside the Capitol without the consent of the other house.

### Joint sessions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Joint sessions")]

Main article: [Joint session of the United States Congress](/wiki/Joint_session_of_the_United_States_Congress "Joint session of the United States Congress")

Joint sessions of the United States Congress occur on special occasions that require a concurrent resolution from House and Senate. These sessions include counting [electoral votes](/wiki/Electoral_votes "Electoral votes") after a presidential election and the president's [State of the Union](/wiki/State_of_the_Union "State of the Union") address. The [constitutionally mandated report](/wiki/State_of_the_Union "State of the Union"), normally given as an annual speech, is modeled on Britain's [Speech from the Throne](/wiki/Speech_from_the_Throne "Speech from the Throne"), was written by most presidents after [Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") but personally delivered as a spoken oration beginning with [Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") in 1913. Joint Sessions and Joint Meetings are traditionally presided over by the speaker of the House, except when counting presidential electoral votes when the vice president (acting as the president of the Senate) presides.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Bills and resolutions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Bills and resolutions")]

See also: [Act of Congress](/wiki/Act_of_Congress "Act of Congress") and [List of United States federal legislation](/wiki/List_of_United_States_federal_legislation "List of United States federal legislation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Private_Law_86-407.jpg/250px-Private_Law_86-407.jpg)](/wiki/File:Private_Law_86-407.jpg)

An [Act of Congress](/wiki/Act_of_Congress "Act of Congress") from 1960

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/US_House_Committee.jpg/250px-US_House_Committee.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_House_Committee.jpg)

The [U.S. Committee on Financial Services](/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_Financial_Services "United States House Committee on Financial Services") with committee members sitting in the tiers of raised chairs and those testifying and audience members sitting below

Ideas for legislation can come from members, lobbyists, state legislatures, constituents, legislative counsel, or executive agencies. Anyone can write a bill, but only members of Congress may introduce bills. Most bills are not written by Congress members, but originate from the Executive branch; interest groups often draft bills as well. The usual next step is for the proposal to be passed to a committee for review.[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11) A proposal is usually in one of these forms:

* Bills are laws in the making. A House-originated bill begins with the letters "H.R." for "House of Representatives", followed by a number kept as it progresses.[[137]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11cc-140)
* Joint resolutions. There is little difference between a bill and a joint resolution since both are treated similarly; a joint resolution originating from the House, for example, begins "H.J.Res." followed by its number.[[137]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11cc-140)
* Concurrent Resolutions affect only the House and Senate and accordingly are not presented to the president. In the House, they begin with "H.Con.Res."[[137]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11cc-140)
* Simple resolutions concern only the House or only the Senate and begin with "H.Res." or "S.Res."[[137]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11cc-140)

Representatives introduce a bill while the House is in session by placing it in the *hopper* on the Clerk's desk.[[137]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11cc-140) It is assigned a number and referred to a committee which studies each bill intensely at this stage.[[137]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11cc-140) Drafting statutes requires "great skill, knowledge, and experience" and sometimes take a year or more.[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11) Sometimes lobbyists write legislation and submit it to a member for introduction. Joint resolutions are the normal way to propose a constitutional amendment or declare war. On the other hand, concurrent resolutions (passed by both houses) and simple resolutions (passed by only one house) do not have the force of law but express the opinion of Congress or regulate [procedure](/wiki/Parliamentary_procedure "Parliamentary procedure"). Bills may be introduced by any member of either house. The Constitution states: "All Bills for raising Revenue shall originate in the House of Representatives." While the Senate cannot originate [revenue](/wiki/Revenue "Revenue") and [appropriation bills](/wiki/Appropriations_bill_(United_States) "Appropriations bill (United States)"), it has the power to amend or reject them. Congress has sought ways to establish appropriate spending levels.[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11)

Each chamber determines its own internal rules of operation unless specified in the Constitution or prescribed by law. In the House, a [Rules Committee](/wiki/U.S._House_Committee_on_Rules "U.S. House Committee on Rules") guides legislation; in the Senate, a [Standing Rules](/wiki/Standing_Rules_of_the_Senate "Standing Rules of the Senate") committee is in charge. Each branch has its own traditions; for example, the Senate relies heavily on the practice of getting "unanimous consent" for noncontroversial matters.[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11) House and Senate rules can be complex, sometimes requiring a hundred specific steps before a bill can become a law.[[11]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14aa-14) Members sometimes turn to outside experts to learn about proper congressional procedures.[[168]](#cite_note-172)

Each bill goes through several stages in each house including consideration by a committee and advice from the [Government Accountability Office](/wiki/Government_Accountability_Office "Government Accountability Office").[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11) Most legislation is considered by [standing committees](/wiki/Standing_committee "Standing committee") which have jurisdiction over a particular subject such as Agriculture or Appropriations. The House has twenty standing committees; the Senate has sixteen. Standing committees meet at least once each month.[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11) Almost all standing committee meetings for transacting business must be open to the public unless the committee votes, publicly, to close the meeting.[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11) A committee might call for public hearings on important bills.[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11) Each committee is led by a [chair](/wiki/Chairperson "Chairperson") who belongs to the majority party and a [ranking member](/wiki/Ranking_minority_member "Ranking minority member") of the minority party. Witnesses and experts can present their case for or against a bill.[[137]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11cc-140) Then, a bill may go to what is called a *mark-up* session, where committee members debate the bill's merits and may offer amendments or revisions.[[137]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11cc-140) Committees may also amend the bill, but the full house holds the power to accept or reject committee amendments. After debate, the committee votes whether it wishes to report the measure to the full house. If a bill is *tabled* then it is rejected. If amendments are extensive, sometimes a new bill with amendments built in will be submitted as a so-called *clean bill* with a new number.[[137]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11cc-140) Both houses have procedures under which committees can be bypassed or overruled but they are rarely used. Generally, members who have been in Congress longer have greater seniority and therefore greater power.[[169]](#cite_note-173)

A bill which reaches the floor of the full house can be simple or complex[[137]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11cc-140) and begins with an [enacting formula](/wiki/Enacting_formula "Enacting formula") such as "Be it enacted by the Senate and House of Representatives of the United States of America in Congress assembled ..." Consideration of a bill requires, itself, a *rule* which is a simple resolution specifying the particulars of debate – time limits, possibility of further amendments, and such.[[137]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11cc-140) Each side has equal time and members can yield to other members who wish to speak.[[137]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11cc-140) Sometimes opponents seek to *recommit* a bill which means to change part of it.[[137]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11cc-140) Generally, discussion requires a *quorum*, usually half of the total number of representatives, before discussion can begin, although there are exceptions.[[170]](#cite_note-174) The house may debate and amend the bill; the precise procedures used by the House and Senate differ. A final vote on the bill follows.

Once a bill is approved by one house, it is sent to the other which may pass, reject, or amend it. For the bill to become law, both houses must agree to identical versions of the bill.[[137]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11cc-140) If the second house amends the bill, then the differences between the two versions must be reconciled in a [conference committee](/wiki/United_States_congressional_conference_committee "United States congressional conference committee"), an *ad hoc* committee that includes senators and representatives[[137]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11cc-140) sometimes by using a *reconciliation process* to limit budget bills.[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11) Both houses use a budget enforcement mechanism informally known as *pay-as-you-go* or *paygo* which discourages members from considering acts that increase budget deficits.[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11) If both houses agree to the version reported by the conference committee, the bill passes, otherwise it fails.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Constitution specifies that a majority of members (a [quorum](/wiki/Quorum "Quorum")) be present before doing business in each house. The rules of each house assume that a quorum is present unless a [quorum call](/wiki/Quorum_call "Quorum call") demonstrates the contrary and debate often continues despite the lack of a majority.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Voting within Congress can take many forms, including systems using lights and bells and electronic voting.[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11) Both houses use voice voting to decide most matters in which members shout "aye" or "no" and the presiding officer announces the result. The Constitution requires a [recorded vote](/wiki/Voting_methods_in_deliberative_assemblies "Voting methods in deliberative assemblies") if demanded by one-fifth of the members present or when voting to override a presidential veto. If the voice vote is unclear or if the matter is controversial, a recorded vote usually happens. The Senate uses [roll-call voting](/wiki/Voting_methods_in_deliberative_assemblies "Voting methods in deliberative assemblies"), in which a clerk calls out the names of all the senators, each senator stating "aye" or "no" when their name is announced. In the Senate, the Vice President may cast the tie-breaking vote if present when the senators are equally divided.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The House reserves roll-call votes for the most formal matters, as a roll call of all 435 representatives takes quite some time; normally, members vote by using an electronic device. In the case of a tie, the motion in question fails. Most votes in the House are done electronically, allowing members to vote *yea* or *nay* or *present* or *open*.[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11) Members insert a voting *ID card* and can change their votes during the last five minutes if they choose; in addition, paper ballots are used occasionally (*yea* indicated by green and *nay* by red).[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11) One member cannot cast a [proxy vote](/wiki/Proxy_vote "Proxy vote") for another.[[8]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11bb-11) Congressional votes are recorded on an online database.[[171]](#cite_note-175)[[172]](#cite_note-176)

After passage by both houses, a bill is [enrolled](/wiki/Enrolled_bill "Enrolled bill") and sent to the president for approval.[[137]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11cc-140) The president may sign it making it law or veto it, perhaps returning it to Congress with the president's objections. A vetoed bill can still become law if each house of Congress votes to override the veto with a two-thirds majority. Finally, the president may do nothing neither signing nor vetoing the bill and then the bill becomes law automatically after ten days (not counting Sundays) according to the Constitution. But if Congress is adjourned during this period, presidents may veto legislation passed at the end of a congressional session simply by ignoring it; the maneuver is known as a [pocket veto](/wiki/Pocket_veto "Pocket veto"), and cannot be overridden by the adjourned Congress.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Public interaction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Public interaction")]

Main article: [U.S. Congress and citizens](/wiki/U.S._Congress_and_citizens "U.S. Congress and citizens")

### Advantage of incumbency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Advantage of incumbency")]

#### Citizens and representatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Citizens and representatives")]

Senators face reelection every six years, and representatives every two. Reelections encourage candidates to focus their publicity efforts at their home states or districts.[[71]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14dd1-74) Running for reelection can be a grueling process of distant travel and fund-raising which distracts senators and representatives from paying attention to governing, according to some critics.[[173]](#cite_note-177) Although others respond that the process is necessary to keep members of Congress in touch with voters.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Incumbent](/wiki/Incumbency "Incumbency") members of Congress running for reelection have strong advantages over challengers.[[60]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11jggha-63) They raise more money[[65]](#cite_note-tws02oct205-68) because donors fund incumbents over challengers, perceiving the former as more likely to win,[[63]](#cite_note-tws02oct223-66)[[174]](#cite_note-tws02oct216-178) and donations are vital for winning elections.[[175]](#cite_note-179) One critic compared election to Congress to receiving [life tenure](/wiki/Life_tenure "Life tenure") at a university.[[174]](#cite_note-tws02oct216-178) Another advantage for representatives is the practice of [gerrymandering](/wiki/Gerrymandering "Gerrymandering").[[176]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11ii-180)[[177]](#cite_note-181) After each ten-year census, states are allocated representatives based on population, and officials in power can choose how to draw the congressional district boundaries to support candidates from their party. As a result, reelection rates of members of Congress hover around 90 percent,[[15]](#cite_note-incumbent-18) causing some critics to call them a privileged class.[[14]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14cc-17) Academics such as Princeton's [Stephen Macedo](/wiki/Stephen_Macedo "Stephen Macedo") have proposed solutions to fix [gerrymandering in the U.S.](/wiki/Gerrymandering_in_the_United_States "Gerrymandering in the United States") Senators and representatives enjoy free mailing privileges, called [franking privileges](/wiki/Franking_privilege "Franking privilege"); while these are not intended for electioneering, this rule is often skirted by borderline election-related mailings during campaigns.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Expensive campaigns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Expensive campaigns")]

In 1971, the cost of running for Congress in [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah") was $70,000[[178]](#cite_note-tws01oct40-182) but costs have climbed.[[179]](#cite_note-tws01oct38-183) The biggest expense is television advertisements.[[64]](#cite_note-tws01oct22-67)[[174]](#cite_note-tws02oct216-178)[[178]](#cite_note-tws01oct40-182)[[180]](#cite_note-tws01oct33-184)[[181]](#cite_note-tws01oct32-185) Today's races cost more than a million dollars for a House seat, and six million or more for a Senate seat.[[14]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14cc-17)[[64]](#cite_note-tws01oct22-67)[[180]](#cite_note-tws01oct33-184)[[182]](#cite_note-186)[[183]](#cite_note-187) Since fundraising is vital, "members of Congress are forced to spend ever-increasing hours raising money for their re-election", according to the Fair Elections Now coalition.[[184]](#cite_note-188)

The Supreme Court has treated campaign contributions as a [free speech](/wiki/Free_speech "Free speech") issue.[[179]](#cite_note-tws01oct38-183) Some see money as a good influence in politics since it "enables candidates to communicate with voters".[[179]](#cite_note-tws01oct38-183) Few members retire from Congress without complaining about how much it costs to campaign for reelection.[[14]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14cc-17) Critics contend that members of Congress are more likely to attend to the needs of heavy campaign contributors than to ordinary citizens.[[14]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14cc-17)

Elections are influenced by many variables. Some political scientists speculate there is a *[coattail effect](/wiki/Coattail_effect "Coattail effect")* (when a popular president or party position has the effect of reelecting incumbents who win by "riding on the president's coattails"), although there is some evidence that the coattail effect is irregular and possibly declining since the 1950s.[[60]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11jggha-63) Some districts are so heavily Democratic or Republican that they are called a [safe seat](/wiki/Safe_seat "Safe seat"); any candidate winning the primary will almost always be elected, and these candidates do not need to spend money on advertising.[[185]](#cite_note-189)[[186]](#cite_note-190) But some races can be competitive when there is no incumbent. If a seat becomes vacant in an open district, then both parties may spend heavily on advertising in these races; in California in 1992, only four of twenty races for House seats were considered highly competitive.[[187]](#cite_note-191)

#### Television and negative advertising

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Television and negative advertising")]

Since members of Congress must advertise heavily on television, this usually involves [negative advertising](/wiki/Negative_advertising "Negative advertising"), which smears an opponent's character without focusing on the issues.[[188]](#cite_note-192) Negative advertising is seen as effective because "the messages tend to stick."[[189]](#cite_note-193) These advertisements sour the public on the political process in general as most members of Congress seek to avoid blame.[[190]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11piiu-194) One wrong decision or one damaging television image can mean defeat at the next election, which leads to a culture of risk avoidance, a need to make policy decisions behind closed doors,[[190]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11piiu-194)[[191]](#cite_note-195) and concentrating publicity efforts in the members' home districts.[[71]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14dd1-74)

#### Perceptions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Perceptions")]

[![Ad for the Federalist.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/An_Advertisement_of_The_Federalist_-_Project_Gutenberg_eText_16960.jpg/250px-An_Advertisement_of_The_Federalist_-_Project_Gutenberg_eText_16960.jpg)](/wiki/File:An_Advertisement_of_The_Federalist_-_Project_Gutenberg_eText_16960.jpg)

*[The Federalist Papers](/wiki/The_Federalist_Papers "The Federalist Papers")*, which argued in favor of a strong connection between citizens and their representatives

Prominent [Founding Fathers](/wiki/Founding_Fathers_of_the_United_States "Founding Fathers of the United States"), writing in *[The Federalist Papers](/wiki/The_Federalist_Papers "The Federalist Papers")*, felt that elections were essential to liberty, that a bond between the people and the representatives was particularly essential,[[192]](#cite_note-tws01oct-196) and that "frequent elections are unquestionably the only policy by which this dependence and sympathy can be effectually secured."[[192]](#cite_note-tws01oct-196) In 2009, few Americans were familiar with leaders of Congress.[[193]](#cite_note-197)[[194]](#cite_note-198)[[195]](#cite_note-199) The percentage of Americans eligible to vote who did, in fact, vote was 63% in 1960, but has been falling since, although there was a slight upward trend in the 2008 election.[[196]](#cite_note-200)

[Public opinion polls](/wiki/Public_opinion_polls "Public opinion polls") asking people if they approve of the job Congress is doing have, in the last few decades, hovered around 25% with some variation.[[14]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14cc-17)[[197]](#cite_note-tws28sep01-201)[[198]](#cite_note-202)[[199]](#cite_note-203)[[200]](#cite_note-204)[[201]](#cite_note-205)[[202]](#cite_note-206) Scholar Julian Zeliger suggested that the "size, messiness, virtues, and vices that make Congress so interesting also create enormous barriers to our understanding the institution ... Unlike the presidency, Congress is difficult to conceptualize."[[203]](#cite_note-207) Other scholars suggest that despite the criticism, "Congress is a remarkably resilient institution ... its place in the political process is not threatened ... it is rich in resources" and that most members behave ethically.[[12]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11rtww-15) They contend that "Congress is easy to dislike and often difficult to defend" and this perception is exacerbated because many challengers running for Congress run *against* Congress, which is an "old form of American politics" that further undermines Congress's reputation with the public:[[14]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14cc-17)

> The rough-and-tumble world of legislating is not orderly and civil, human frailties too often taint its membership, and legislative outcomes are often frustrating and ineffective ... Still, we are not exaggerating when we say that Congress is essential to American democracy. We would not have survived as a nation without a Congress that represented the diverse interests of our society, conducted a public debate on the major issues, found compromises to resolve conflicts peacefully, and limited the power of our executive, military, and judicial institutions ... The popularity of Congress ebbs and flows with the public's confidence in government generally ... the legislative process is easy to dislike – it often generates political posturing and grandstanding, it necessarily involves compromise, and it often leaves broken promises in its trail. Also, members of Congress often appear self-serving as they pursue their political careers and represent interests and reflect values that are controversial. Scandals, even when they involve a single member, add to the public's frustration with Congress and have contributed to the institution's low ratings in opinion polls.

— Smith, Roberts & Wielen[[14]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14cc-17)

An additional factor that confounds public perceptions of Congress is that congressional issues are becoming more technical and complex and require expertise in subjects such as science, engineering and economics.[[14]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14cc-17) As a result, Congress often cedes authority to experts at the executive branch.[[14]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14cc-17)

Since 2006, Congress has dropped ten points in the Gallup confidence poll with only nine percent having "a great deal" or "quite a lot" of confidence in their legislators.[[204]](#cite_note-208) Since 2011, [Gallup poll](/wiki/Gallup_poll "Gallup poll") has reported Congress's approval rating among Americans at 10% or below three times.[[77]](#cite_note-blogs.wsj.com-80)[[78]](#cite_note-firstread.nbcnews.com-81) Public opinion of Congress plummeted further to 5% in October 2013 after parts of the U.S. government deemed 'nonessential government' shut down.[[79]](#cite_note-voa5congress-82)

### Smaller states and bigger states

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Smaller states and bigger states")]

When the Constitution was ratified in 1787, the ratio of the populations of large states to small states was roughly twelve to one. The [Connecticut Compromise](/wiki/Connecticut_Compromise "Connecticut Compromise") gave every state, large and small, an equal vote in the Senate.[[205]](#cite_note-tws10janx01-209) Since each state has two senators, residents of smaller states have more clout in the Senate than residents of larger states. But since 1787, the population disparity between large and small states has grown; in 2006, for example, [California](/wiki/California "California") had seventy times the population of [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming").[[206]](#cite_note-210)

Critics, such as constitutional scholar [Sanford Levinson](/wiki/Sanford_Levinson "Sanford Levinson"), have suggested that the population disparity works against residents of large states and causes a steady redistribution of resources from "large states to small states".[[207]](#cite_note-211)[[208]](#cite_note-212)[[209]](#cite_note-213) Others argue that the Connecticut Compromise was deliberately intended by the Founding Fathers to construct the Senate so that each state had equal footing not based on population,[[205]](#cite_note-tws10janx01-209) and contend that the result works well on balance.

### Members and constituents

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Members and constituents")]

A major role for members of Congress is providing services to [constituents](/wiki/Constituency "Constituency").[[210]](#cite_note-214) Constituents request assistance with problems.[[211]](#cite_note-215) Providing services helps members of Congress win votes and elections[[176]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11ii-180)[[212]](#cite_note-216)[[213]](#cite_note-217) and can make a difference in close races.[[214]](#cite_note-218) Congressional staff can help citizens navigate government bureaucracies.[[11]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14aa-14) One academic described the complex intertwined relation between lawmakers and constituents as *home style*.[[215]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14bb-219): 8

### Motivation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Motivation")]

One way to categorize lawmakers, according to former [University of Rochester](/wiki/University_of_Rochester "University of Rochester") political science professor [Richard Fenno](/wiki/Richard_Fenno "Richard Fenno"), is by their general motivation:

1. Reelection: These are lawmakers who "never met a voter they didn't like" and provide excellent constituent services.
2. Good public policy: Legislators who "burnish a reputation for policy expertise and leadership".
3. Power in the chamber: Lawmakers who spend serious time along the "rail of the House floor or in the Senate cloakroom ministering to the needs of their colleagues". Famous legislator [Henry Clay](/wiki/Henry_Clay "Henry Clay") in the mid-19th century was described as an "issue entrepreneur" who looked for issues to serve his ambitions.[[215]](#cite_note-tws2010Sep11t14bb-219): 34

## Privileges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Privileges")]

See also: [United States House of Representatives § Salary and benefits](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives#Salary_and_benefits "United States House of Representatives"), and [United States Senate § Salary and benefits](/wiki/United_States_Senate#Salary_and_benefits "United States Senate")

### Outside income and gifts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Outside income and gifts")]

See also: [Revolving door (politics)](/wiki/Revolving_door_(politics) "Revolving door (politics)"), [Supreme Court of the United States § Ethics](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States#Ethics "Supreme Court of the United States"), and [McDonnell v. United States](/wiki/McDonnell_v._United_States "McDonnell v. United States")

Representative [Jim Cooper](/wiki/Jim_Cooper "Jim Cooper") of [Tennessee](/wiki/Tennessee "Tennessee") told Harvard professor [Lawrence Lessig](/wiki/Lawrence_Lessig "Lawrence Lessig") that a chief problem with Congress was that members focused on their future careers as lobbyists after serving – that Congress was a "[Farm League](/wiki/Farm_team "Farm team") for [K Street](/wiki/K_Street_(Washington,_D.C.) "K Street (Washington, D.C.)")".[[216]](#cite_note-220)[[217]](#cite_note-221) Family members of active legislators have also been hired by lobbying firms, which while not allowed to lobby their family member, has drawn criticism as a conflict of interest.[[218]](#cite_note-222)

Members of congress have been accused of [insider trading](/wiki/Insider_trading "Insider trading"), such as in the [2020 congressional insider trading scandal](/wiki/2020_congressional_insider_trading_scandal "2020 congressional insider trading scandal"), where members of Congress or their family members have traded on stocks related to work on their committees.[[219]](#cite_note-223) One 2011 study concluded that portfolios of members of Congress outperformed both the market and hedge funds, which the authors suggested as evidence of insider trading.[[220]](#cite_note-224) Proposed solutions include putting stocks in blind trusts to prevent future insider trading.[[221]](#cite_note-225)

Some members of Congress have gone on lavish trips paid for by outside groups, sometimes bringing family members, which are often legal even if in an ethical gray area.[[222]](#cite_note-226)[[223]](#cite_note-227)

### Pay

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Pay")]

See also: [Salaries of members of the United States Congress](/wiki/Salaries_of_members_of_the_United_States_Congress "Salaries of members of the United States Congress")

Some critics complain congressional pay is high compared with a median American [income](/wiki/Household_income_in_the_United_States "Household income in the United States").[[224]](#cite_note-228) Others have countered that congressional pay is consistent with other [branches of government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States").[[197]](#cite_note-tws28sep01-201) Another criticism is that members of Congress are insulated from the [health care market](/wiki/Health_insurance_marketplace "Health insurance marketplace") due to their coverage.[[225]](#cite_note-229) Others have criticized the wealth of members of Congress.[[178]](#cite_note-tws01oct40-182)[[181]](#cite_note-tws01oct32-185) In January 2014, it was reported that for the first time over half of the members of Congress were millionaires.[[226]](#cite_note-230) Congress has been criticized for trying to conceal pay raises by slipping them into a large bill at the last minute.[[227]](#cite_note-231)

Members elected since 1984 are covered by the [Federal Employees Retirement System](/wiki/Federal_Employees_Retirement_System "Federal Employees Retirement System") (FERS). Like other federal employees, congressional retirement is funded through taxes and participants' contributions. Members of Congress under FERS contribute 1.3% of their salary into the FERS retirement plan and pay 6.2% of their salary in Social Security taxes. And like federal employees, members contribute one-third of the cost of health insurance with the government covering the other two-thirds.[[228]](#cite_note-232) The size of a congressional pension depends on the years of service and the average of the highest three years of their salary. By law, the starting amount of a member's retirement annuity may not exceed 80% of their final salary. In 2018, the average annual pension for retired senators and representatives under the [Civil Service Retirement System](/wiki/Civil_Service_Retirement_System "Civil Service Retirement System") (CSRS) was $75,528, while those who retired under FERS, or in combination with CSRS, was $41,208.[[229]](#cite_note-233)

Members of Congress make fact-finding missions to learn about other countries and stay informed, but these outings can cause controversy if the trip is deemed excessive or unconnected with the task of governing. For example, *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")* reported in 2009 that lawmaker trips abroad at taxpayer expense had included spas, $300-per-night extra unused rooms, and shopping excursions.[[230]](#cite_note-tws17decaafds-234) Some lawmakers responded that "traveling with spouses compensates for being away from them a lot in Washington" and justify the trips as a way to meet officials in other nations.[[230]](#cite_note-tws17decaafds-234)

By the [Twenty-seventh Amendment](/wiki/Twenty-seventh_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Twenty-seventh Amendment to the United States Constitution"), changes to congressional pay may not take effect before the next election to the House of Representatives.[[231]](#cite_note-Ronald_Reagan-235) In *Boehner v. Anderson*, the [United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit](/wiki/United_States_Court_of_Appeals_for_the_District_of_Columbia_Circuit "United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit") ruled that the amendment does not affect [cost-of-living adjustments](/wiki/Cost-of-living_index "Cost-of-living index").[[232]](#cite_note-236)[[231]](#cite_note-Ronald_Reagan-235)

### Postage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Postage")]

The [franking](/wiki/Franking "Franking") privilege allows members of Congress to send official mail to constituents at government expense. Though they are not permitted to send election materials, borderline material is often sent, especially in the run-up to an election by those in close races.[[233]](#cite_note-237)[[234]](#cite_note-238) Some academics consider free mailings as giving incumbents a big advantage over challengers.[[15]](#cite_note-incumbent-18)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[235]](#cite_note-239)

### Protection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=United_States_Congress&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Protection")]

Members of Congress enjoy [parliamentary privilege](/wiki/Parliamentary_privilege "Parliamentary privilege"), including [freedom from arrest](/wiki/Parliamentary_immunity "Parliamentary immunity") in all cases except for [treason](/wiki/Treason "Treason"), [felony](/wiki/Felony "Felony"), and [breach of the peace](/wiki/Breach_of_the_peace "Breach of the peace"), and [freedom of speech](/wiki/Freedom_of_speech "Freedom of speech") in debate. This constitutionally derived [immunity](/wiki/Congressional_immunity "Congressional immunity") applies to members during sessions and when traveling to and from sessions.[[236]](#cite_note-240) The term "arrest" has been interpreted broadly, and includes any detention or delay in the course of [law enforcement](/wiki/Police "Police"), including [court summons](/wiki/Summons "Summons") and [subpoenas](/wiki/Subpoena "Subpoena"). The rules of the House strictly guard this privilege; a member may not waive the privilege on their own but must seek the permission of the whole house to do so. Senate rules are less strict and permit individual senators to waive the privilege as they choose.[[237]](#cite_note-241)

The Constitution guarantees absolute freedom of debate in both houses, providing in the [Speech or Debate Clause](/wiki/Speech_or_Debate_Clause "Speech or Debate Clause") of the Constitution that "for any Speech or Debate in either House, they shall not be questioned in any other Place." Accordingly, a member of Congress may not be sued in court for [slander](/wiki/Slander "Slander") because of remarks made in either house, although each house has its own rules restricting offensive speeches, and may punish members who transgress.[[238]](#cite_note-242)

Obstructing the work of Congress is a crime under [federal law](/wiki/Federal_law "Federal law") and is known as [contempt of Congress](/wiki/Contempt_of_Congress "Contempt of Congress"). Each member has the power to cite people for contempt but can only issue a contempt citation – the judicial system pursues the matter like a normal criminal case. If convicted in court of contempt of Congress, a person may be imprisoned for up to one year.[[239]](#cite_note-243)
